---
title: "Reflection"
---

## Looking back to move forward

This module has been one of the most technically demanding — and rewarding — experiences of my MSc journey. Coming from a background in traditional banking risk management, I was comfortable with statistical models and data analysis, but the tools and workflows covered here were largely new to me.

::: {.callout-note title="My starting point"}
Before this module I had never used Git, Quarto, or built a website from scratch. My technical work had always lived in SAS, Excel, and internal banking systems — never in the open web.
:::

---

## What I learned

### Git and version control

Learning Git was a mindset shift. In banking, version control meant saving files as `model_v2_FINAL_final.sas`. Git taught me to think incrementally — every commit tells a story of how a project evolved. I now understand why software teams treat commit history as documentation.

The most valuable lesson: **commit often, commit descriptively**. A clear commit message is a gift to your future self.

### Quarto and web publishing

Quarto surprised me. I expected a simple document tool, but it's a complete publishing system. The ability to combine narrative, executable code, and professional design in a single `.qmd` file is genuinely powerful — especially for a risk analyst who needs to communicate findings to both technical and non-technical audiences.

### Python for financial analysis

My Python skills have grown significantly. Before this module I could write basic scripts; now I can build end-to-end analytical pipelines with visualisations, from data simulation to model evaluation. Working with `scikit-learn` and `plotly` in a reproducible environment has changed how I think about analysis.

### Web design and SCSS

I never expected to enjoy CSS. But learning to build a cohesive visual identity — choosing a colour palette, configuring typography, making design decisions — gave me a new appreciation for the communication dimension of data work. A well-designed portfolio is not vanity; it is clarity.

---

## Challenges I overcame

| Challenge | How I solved it |
|-----------|----------------|
| Git conflicts and errors | Reading error messages carefully, using `git status` constantly |
| Quarto SCSS not rendering | Understanding the `scss:defaults` / `scss:rules` layer structure |
| Python environment issues | Installing dependencies incrementally, reading stack traces |
| Impostor syndrome | Remembering that every expert was once a beginner |

---

## Connecting to my future goals

I want to work in a fintech — somewhere like Revolut, Nubank, or Stripe — in a role that combines credit risk expertise with modern data engineering. This module has shown me that the gap between "banking risk analyst" and "fintech data professional" is smaller than I thought. The fundamentals are the same; what changes is the tooling and the pace.

::: {.callout-tip title="Key insight"}
The skills I built here — reproducible analysis, version control, web publishing — are exactly what modern risk teams at fintechs use. This portfolio is not just an academic exercise; it is a professional signal.
:::

Building this portfolio has also reinforced something I believe deeply: **high standards matter**. Every page, every commit, every line of code reflects how you work. I hold myself to high standards not because I was told to, but because I believe the quality of your work is the clearest signal of your professionalism.

---

## What comes next

- Deepen my machine learning knowledge — particularly in survival analysis and credit loss forecasting
- Contribute to open source risk analytics tools
- Apply for risk analytics roles at fintechs in Ireland and the UK
- Keep building in public — this portfolio will continue to grow beyond this module

---

## Further learning

A talk that has shaped how I think about the future of credit risk and fintech:

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/fpFCBqVxFPc" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>
